Consistency.justification: The proposal demonstrates excellent alignment with all provided inputs. It directly addresses the workshop's focus on 'Next Generation of Sequence Modeling Architectures', specifically targeting the limitations of State Space Models (SSMs) regarding In-Context Learning (ICL) and memory. It faithfully expands the provided research idea of 'Contextual State Reinstatement' (CSR) and integrates the literature review's insights on the link between Transformer ICL and Cognitive Memory Theory (CMR).

Clarity.justification: The proposal is generally very clear and well-structured. The mathematical formulation of the CSR mechanism is explicit. However, there is a minor ambiguity regarding the 'sliding context window' mentioned in section 2.1.3. The mathematical formulation (c_t = \\alpha c_{t-1} + ...) implies a purely recurrent state with implicit exponential decay, whereas the text suggests an explicit window W to maintain bounded memory. It is unclear if this window implies a hard cutoff or a buffer, which could affect the complexity or implementation details.

Novelty.justification: The proposal is highly innovative in its approach to bridging cognitive psychology (CMR model) with modern efficient neural architectures (Mamba). While hybrid architectures (MambaFormer) exist, attempting to engineer cognitive memory mechanisms directly into the SSM state dynamics to solve ICL without attention is a novel and distinct contribution.

Soundness.justification: The theoretical foundation is strong, leveraging recent findings by Ji-An et al. (2024). The methodology is rigorous, with appropriate baselines and ablation studies. However, there is a theoretical risk regarding the capacity of the proposed mechanism. The CSR mechanism relies on a vector-based context c_t and a similarity gate to 'reinstate' context. In-Context Learning often requires precise retrieval of specific past tokens (associative memory), and it is debatable whether adding a summary context vector c_t back into the state is sufficient to emulate the 'jump back in time' precision of attention heads. Nevertheless, it is a valid hypothesis to test.

Feasibility.justification: The proposal is practical. The architectural modifications are composed of standard operations (MLPs, gating, element-wise addition) and preserve the linear complexity of Mamba. The training scale (370M to 1.4B parameters) is ambitious but standard for this type of research and feasible given appropriate compute resources. The evaluation plan is concrete and achievable.

Significance.justification: The problem addressed—the ICL gap between SSMs and Transformers—is one of the most critical open problems in sequence modeling. If successful, this research would unlock efficient, linear-time models capable of strong in-context learning, having a major impact on the field. The interdisciplinary link to cognitive science adds further value.

strengths: Strong theoretical motivation connecting cognitive science with deep learning architectures.
Addresses a critical and timely limitation of State Space Models (ICL performance).
Proposed solution preserves the O(n) efficiency advantage of SSMs.
Comprehensive evaluation plan including behavioral signatures to validate the mechanism.
weaknesses: The vector-based context reinstatement mechanism may lack the capacity for precise token retrieval compared to key-value attention mechanisms.
Minor ambiguity in the description of the sliding window implementation versus the recurrent mathematical formulation.
